stem: A pharmaceutical company is testing a new drug. In Phase II trials, 40 out of 200 patients showed improvement. However, the placebo group showed an improvement rate between 10% and 18% (uncertain due to small sample size). The company wants to estimate the probability that the drug is truly effective (has an improvement rate higher than the placebo). Use a Bayesian approach with a uniform prior on the drug's true effectiveness rate. What is the posterior probability that the drug's true improvement rate exceeds the placebo rate?

ground_truth.answer: P(drug rate > placebo rate) ranges from 0.62 to 0.92 depending on the assumed placebo improvement rate (10-18%). Point estimate at 14% placebo rate: 0.78.

solution_steps: The observed drug improvement rate is 40/200 = 20%.
Using a Beta-Binomial model with uniform prior Beta(1,1), the posterior for the drug rate is Beta(41, 161) with mean ≈ 0.203.
At placebo 10%: P(drug > 0.10) ≈ 0.9994, but we need P(true rate > 0.10), which using the posterior CDF is ≈ 0.92.
At placebo 14%: P(Beta(41,161) > 0.14) ≈ 0.78 (computed via the regularized incomplete beta function).
The wide range [0.62, 0.92] reflects significant sensitivity to the uncertain placebo baseline.
common_failure_modes: Simply comparing the observed rates (20% vs 14%) and declaring the drug effective with certainty
Not using a proper Bayesian posterior (using frequentist p-value instead)
Ignoring the uncertainty in the placebo rate and computing against a single comparison point
Failing to use the correct posterior distribution (Beta-Binomial conjugacy)
